title: Quality, Six Sigma & Continuous Improvement
simple_html:
Quality in operations means consistently giving customers what they expect — a product or service that is fit for purpose and free of defects. Managing quality is not just inspecting at the end; it is designing and running processes so that good results happen reliably, every time.

Two ways to think about quality

  • Meeting specification — the product does what it was designed to do, within agreed limits ("conformance").
  • Meeting expectations — the customer is satisfied; quality is the gap between what they expected and what they got.

The big idea: reduce variation

Every process varies a little. Too much variation means some output falls outside acceptable limits — defects. The heart of modern quality management is reducing variation so the process stays comfortably inside the limits. Six Sigma is a famous, data-driven programme built on exactly this: "six sigma" is a statistical target of about 3.4 defects per million opportunities — almost perfect.

Continuous improvement

Quality is never "finished." Continuous improvement (the Japanese kaizen) means making lots of small, ongoing improvements rather than waiting for one big fix. The engine is the PDCA cycle — Plan, Do, Check, Act — repeated forever: try a change, see if it worked, lock it in or adjust, then go again.

Key takeaways

  • Quality means conformance to spec and meeting customer expectations.
  • Prevention beats inspection — build quality in rather than catching defects later.
  • Reducing process variation is the core technical goal; Six Sigma targets ~3.4 defects per million.
  • Six Sigma projects follow DMAIC: Define, Measure, Analyse, Improve, Control.
  • Improvement is continuous (kaizen / PDCA), driven by data, and owned by everyone.
intermediate_html:
Quality management is the set of activities by which an organisation ensures its products and services consistently meet customer requirements and improve over time. This lesson links three connected ideas: what quality is, the statistical discipline of Six Sigma for reducing defects, and the culture of continuous improvement that sustains gains.

Defining and dimensioning quality

Garvin (1987) identifies eight dimensions of product quality — performance, features, reliability, conformance, durability, serviceability, aesthetics, and perceived quality — while for services the SERVQUAL model (Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry, 1988) uses reliability, assurance, tangibles, empathy, and responsiveness. The operational workhorse is conformance to specification: producing within defined tolerance limits. Crucially, the cost of quality has two faces: costs of conformance (prevention and appraisal) versus costs of non-conformance (internal and external failure). The insight (Crosby, 1979, "quality is free") is that spending on prevention usually pays back many times over by avoiding far larger failure costs.

From inspection to prevention: SPC

Statistical Process Control (SPC), pioneered by Shewhart (1931), distinguishes common-cause variation (inherent, random, in-control) from special-cause variation (assignable, out-of-control). A control chart plots a process metric over time with a centre line and upper/lower control limits (typically +/-3 standard deviations); points outside the limits or non-random patterns signal special causes to investigate. The goal is a process that is first stable (in statistical control) and then capable (comfortably within specification).

Process capability

Capability indices compare the voice of the process (its spread) with the voice of the customer (the spec). Cp = (USL - LSL) / (6 sigma) measures potential capability; Cpk adjusts for centring. A Cpk of 1.0 means the spec limits sit at +/-3 sigma; 1.33 is a common minimum and 2.0 corresponds to true Six Sigma performance.

Six Sigma and DMAIC

Six Sigma, developed at Motorola (1986) and scaled famously by GE under Jack Welch, is a data-driven methodology to reduce variation and defects to about 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO) — which, allowing for a 1.5-sigma long-term drift, corresponds to a 6-sigma process. Improvement projects follow the DMAIC roadmap:

PhasePurpose
DefineScope the problem, customer (CTQ) requirements, goals
MeasureQuantify current performance; validate the measurement system
AnalyseFind root causes with data (Pareto, fishbone, hypothesis tests)
ImproveDesign and pilot solutions that address root causes
ControlLock in gains with SPC, standards, and monitoring

Roles are organised by martial-arts "belts" (Green Belt, Black Belt, Master Black Belt) sponsored by Champions.

Worked example

A filling line must deliver 500 +/-9 ml (LSL 491, USL 509). Measured output has mean 502 ml and standard deviation 2 ml. Cp = (509 - 491)/(6 x 2) = 18/12 = 1.5. But the process is off-centre: Cpk = min[(509 - 502)/(3 x 2), (502 - 491)/(3 x 2)] = min[7/6, 11/6] = min[1.17, 1.83] = 1.17. The process could be capable (Cp 1.5) but its off-centring drops real capability below the 1.33 target — the fix is to recentre the mean toward 500, instantly raising Cpk to 1.5. This shows why centring matters as much as spread.

Continuous improvement and key thinkers

The improvement culture rests on the PDCA cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act, the Shewhart/Deming cycle) and kaizen. W. Edwards Deming's 14 Points and emphasis on systems thinking, Joseph Juran's trilogy (planning, control, improvement) and Pareto principle, and Kaoru Ishikawa's tools (the fishbone/cause-and-effect diagram, quality circles) form the canon. Total Quality Management (TQM) integrates these into a company-wide, customer-focused, everyone-involved approach.

How to apply it

Diagnose whether a problem is a stability issue (special causes — use control charts) or a capability issue (reduce spread or recentre). For a structured fix, run a DMAIC project: define CTQs, measure baseline DPMO and Cpk, analyse root causes with Pareto and fishbone, improve, then control with SPC. Pair Six Sigma's variation reduction with lean's waste reduction ("Lean Six Sigma") for both speed and quality.
full_html:
Quality, Six Sigma, and continuous improvement together address the central question of how an organisation produces reliably good outcomes and keeps getting better. Quality management has evolved through four eras — inspection, statistical quality control, quality assurance, and total/strategic quality management — moving steadily from "catch defects at the end" to "design and run processes so defects do not occur." This lesson synthesises the definitions of quality, the statistical machinery of process control and capability, the Six Sigma improvement methodology, and the philosophy of continuous improvement, with links to lean (Lesson 7) and process design (Lesson 4).

What is quality? Competing definitions

Garvin (1984) catalogues five approaches: transcendent (innate excellence), product-based (measurable attributes), user-based (fitness for use — Juran), manufacturing-based (conformance to specification — Crosby), and value-based (quality relative to price). His eight dimensions of quality (Garvin, 1987) — performance, features, reliability, conformance, durability, serviceability, aesthetics, perceived quality — show quality is multidimensional and strategic, not a single number. For services, the dominant model is SERVQUAL (Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry, 1988), which frames service quality as the gap between customer expectations and perceptions across reliability, assurance, tangibles, empathy, and responsiveness — the "gaps model." Operationally, the most actionable definition is conformance to specification, because it is measurable and controllable.

The cost of quality

Feigenbaum's (1956) cost-of-quality framework partitions quality costs into four buckets: prevention (training, design, process control) and appraisal (inspection, testing) — together the costs of conformance — versus internal failure (scrap, rework before delivery) and external failure (warranty, returns, lost reputation) — the costs of non-conformance. Crosby's (1979) provocative claim that "quality is free" rests on the empirical pattern that modest investment in prevention yields disproportionate reductions in failure costs, which typically dwarf prevention and appraisal spending. The managerial implication is to shift spending leftward — from appraisal and failure toward prevention.

Statistical process control and the two types of variation

Walter Shewhart (1931) at Bell Labs founded SPC on a profound distinction, later central to Deming's philosophy: common-cause (chance) variation is the inherent, random noise of a stable process, whereas special-cause (assignable) variation arises from specific, identifiable disturbances. Confusing the two causes tampering — Deming's famous funnel experiment shows that adjusting a process in response to common-cause noise actually increases variation. A control chart (X-bar/R, p, c, etc.) plots a statistic over time with a centre line and control limits at +/-3 standard deviations of the sampling distribution. Rules (a point beyond the limits, runs, trends) flag special causes for investigation while leaving common-cause variation alone. A process exhibiting only common-cause variation is "in statistical control" — predictable, but not necessarily good enough.

Process capability and centring

Stability is necessary but not sufficient; the process must also be capable of meeting specification. Capability indices compare process spread (voice of the process) with tolerance width (voice of the customer): Cp = (USL - LSL)/(6 sigma) measures potential capability assuming perfect centring, while Cpk = min[(USL - mu)/(3 sigma), (mu - LSL)/(3 sigma)] penalises off-centre processes. When Cp = Cpk the process is centred; when Cpk < Cp it is off-target. Benchmarks: Cpk = 1.0 places spec limits at +/-3 sigma (about 2,700 DPMO); 1.33 is a widely required minimum; 2.0 corresponds to Six Sigma (spec limits at +/-6 sigma). This is precisely the concept tested in the worked example below.

Six Sigma: origin, statistics, and DMAIC

Six Sigma was developed at Motorola by Bill Smith and Mikel Harry (1986) and propelled to fame by General Electric under Jack Welch in the late 1990s, who tied it to executive compensation. Statistically, a "six sigma" process has six standard deviations between the mean and the nearest specification limit. Allowing for an empirically observed long-term mean drift of 1.5 sigma, this corresponds to 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO) — the headline number. The improvement engine for existing processes is DMAIC: Define (problem, scope, customer critical-to-quality requirements), Measure (baseline performance, gauge R&R to validate the measurement system), Analyse (root-cause analysis using Pareto charts, Ishikawa/fishbone diagrams, regression and hypothesis testing), Improve (pilot data-backed solutions, often with designed experiments), and Control (sustain via SPC, standard work, and control plans). For new designs, the analogue is DMADV/DFSS (Design for Six Sigma). Organisationally, Six Sigma uses a belt hierarchy — Yellow, Green, and Black Belts, Master Black Belts, and executive Champions/Sponsors — to embed analytical capability and governance.

Worked calculation

A bottling process targets 500 ml with specification 500 +/-12 ml (LSL = 488, USL = 512). A sample yields mean = 505 ml and sigma = 2.5 ml. Then Cp = (512 - 488)/(6 x 2.5) = 24/15 = 1.60 (excellent potential). But Cpk = min[(512 - 505)/(3 x 2.5), (505 - 488)/(3 x 2.5)] = min[7/7.5, 17/7.5] = min[0.93, 2.27] = 0.93 — below the 1.33 standard, driven entirely by the 5 ml off-centring. Recentring the mean to 500 ml would raise Cpk to equal Cp (1.60) with no change in variation, eliminating most defects at essentially zero cost. The defect rate at Cpk 0.93 (~ z of 2.8 on the near side) is several thousand DPMO; at Cpk 1.60 it is a handful. This demonstrates the lesson's core teaching: both spread and centring govern capability, and centring is often the cheaper lever.

Continuous improvement: PDCA, kaizen, and the quality gurus

Sustained quality depends on a culture of continuous improvement. The PDCA cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act) — originated by Shewhart and championed by Deming (who called it PDSA, Study rather than Check) — institutionalises learning as repeated, evidence-based experimentation. Kaizen (Imai, 1986) emphasises many small, employee-driven improvements over occasional large investments. The canonical thinkers: W. Edwards Deming (1986) — 14 Points for Management, the System of Profound Knowledge, attack on tampering and on slogans, drive out fear; Joseph Juran (1951) — the Quality Trilogy (planning, control, improvement), fitness for use, and the Pareto principle ("vital few vs trivial many"); Philip Crosby (1979) — zero defects, do it right first time, quality is free; Kaoru Ishikawa — the cause-and-effect (fishbone) diagram, the seven basic quality tools, and quality circles; and Genichi Taguchi — the quality loss function, which holds that any deviation from target causes a loss that grows quadratically, sharpening the case for centring over mere conformance. Total Quality Management (TQM) integrates these into a company-wide, customer-centred system of continuous improvement involving every employee.

Lean Six Sigma and the integration with lean

Lean (Lesson 7) attacks waste and speed while Six Sigma attacks variation and defects; the two are complementary, and Lean Six Sigma fuses them — reducing variation makes low buffers safe, and reducing waste shortens the cycle in which quality is built. Jidoka's "stop the line for quality" is itself a quality-at-source practice.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Tampering — adjusting a stable process in response to common-cause noise, which Deming proved increases variation.
  • Confusing control limits with specification limits — control limits describe the process's actual voice; spec limits express the customer's requirement. They are independent.
  • Chasing Cp while ignoring Cpk — a process can have great potential capability yet produce defects because it is off-centre.
  • Inspecting quality in — relying on end-of-line appraisal rather than prevention; you cannot inspect quality into a product.
  • Treating Six Sigma as a tool kit, not a system — projects without management commitment, CTQ linkage, and a control phase tend to regress.
  • Goal displacement — over-quantification and belt bureaucracy can stifle the very experimentation kaizen requires.

Debates

Critics (notably during 3M's experience under McNerney) argue Six Sigma's emphasis on incremental, defect-focused optimisation can crowd out radical innovation, which thrives on tolerated failure and variability. Others question the empirical basis of the 1.5-sigma shift. The reconciling view places Six Sigma where requirements are stable and measurable, and protects exploratory innovation under different metrics.

Cross-links

Quality is one of the five performance objectives (Lesson 2) and a potential order-winner in operations strategy (Lesson 3); SPC and capability operate on the processes designed in Lesson 4; quality-at-source is a pillar of lean/JIT (Lesson 7); and failure prevention and reliability connect to managing failure and risk (Lesson 9).
